coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a novel electrochemical aptasensor assisted by a novel computerized monitoring system for real-time determination of the prostate specific antigen: A computerized experimental method brought elegancy
coredata.dc:creator: Soleimani, Shokoufeh
Arkan, Elham
Jalalvand, Ali R.
Goicoechea, Hector C.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel and efficient aptasensor assisted by a computerized monitoring system namely SACMES was introduced for real time determination of the prostate specific antigen (PSA). The platform of the aptasensor was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with graphene quantum dots-chitosan-nafion-ionic liquid/multiwalled carbon nanotubes-graphene-ionic liquid/polypyrrole-MoS2-ionic liquid-AuPt nanoparticles/aptamer (APT/Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE). After characterization of the steps of the modification process used to fabricate the aptasensor by electrochemical and microscopic techniques, its square wave voltammetric (SWV) responses at different frequencies as a large-volume of electrochemical data were calibrated with the help of SACMES as an efficient software which helped us to develop an interesting method for real time determination of the PSA. Our records confirmed that the aptasensor response was linearly correlated with PSA concentration in the range of 0.5–350 pg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 pg mL−1. Our results also confirmed that aptasensor was stable, repeatable, reproducible, selective and sensitive for determination of the PSA and its performance in the analysis of real samples was acceptable.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aptasensor
Real time determination
PSA
SACMES

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Examination of different aptasensors for selecting the best one for detection of the PSA
Examination of the interference effects on aptasensor response
Stability, repeatability and reproducibility of the aptasensor
Application of the aptasensor to the analysis of the real samples
Comparison with the previous works reported in the literature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images taken from the surface of (A) GCE, (B) GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (C) MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (D) Ppy/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (E) Ppy-MoS2/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE and (F) Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE. (G) EDS results for elemental analysis of S, Mo, Au and Pt.

(A) EISs and (B) CVs of different electrodes in contact with 0.05 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− where (a) GCE, (b) GQDs-NAF/GCE, (c) GQDs-IL/GCE, (d) GQDs-NAF-IL/GCE, (e) GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (f) MWCNTs- IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (g) MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (h) Ppy/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (i) Ppy-MoS2-IL/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, (j) Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE and (k) APT/Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE.
(A) SWV responses of the aptasensor to increasing concentration of the PSA in the range of 0.5–350 pg mL−1, (B) calculated AUC of the samples and (C) variation of the AUC versus concentration of PSA.
A brief description of the steps of the work reported in this article.
Application of the fabricated aptasensor to the analysis of real samples.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a novel electrochemical aptasensor assisted by a novel computerized monitoring system for real-time determination of the prostate specific antigen: A computerized experimental method brought elegancy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Research Institute for Health Technology, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aNano Drug Delivery Research Center, Research Institute for Health Technology, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Oils and Fats, Research Institute for Health Technology, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Oils and Fats

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bResearch Center of Oils and Fats, Research Institute for Health Technology, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratorio de Desarrollo Analítico y Quimiometría (LADAQ), Cátedra de Química Analítica I, Universidad Nacional del Litoral, Ciudad Universitaria, CC 242, S3000ZAA Santa Fe, Argentina

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratorio de Desarrollo Analítico y Quimiometría (LADAQ)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cLaboratorio de Desarrollo Analítico y Quimiometría (LADAQ), Cátedra de Química Analítica I, Universidad Nacional del Litoral, Ciudad Universitaria, CC 242, S3000ZAA Santa Fe, Argentina

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel and efficient aptasensor assisted by a computerized monitoring system namely SACMES was introduced for real time determination of the prostate specific antigen (PSA). The platform of the aptasensor was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with graphene quantum dots-chitosan-nafion-ionic liquid/multiwalled carbon nanotubes-graphene-ionic liquid/polypyrrole-MoS2-ionic liquid-AuPt nanoparticles/aptamer (APT/Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE). After characterization of the steps of the modification process used to fabricate the aptasensor by electrochemical and microscopic techniques, its square wave voltammetric (SWV) responses at different frequencies as a large-volume of electrochemical data were calibrated with the help of SACMES as an efficient software which helped us to develop an interesting method for real time determination of the PSA. Our records confirmed that the aptasensor response was linearly correlated with PSA concentration in the range of 0.5–350 pg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.14 pg mL−1. Our results also confirmed that aptasensor was stable, repeatable, reproducible, selective and sensitive for determination of the PSA and its performance in the analysis of real samples was acceptable.

ce:section.ce:para: Prostate specific antigen (PSA) is a very important biomarker which is widely used to detect prostate cancer [1]. Prostate cancer as a malignant tumor which is found in prostate is the third main cause of cancer mortality in men [2]. Accurate and early diagnosis of cancer can increase the chances of its successful treatment therefore, developing a facile and accurate approach for PSA detection is very important for clinical early-stage diagnosis of prostate cancer [3].

Previous works have reported a variety of analytical methods such as radioimmunoassay [4], fluorescence [5], chemiluminescence [6], surface-enhanced Raman scattering-based immunoassay [7] and plasmonic enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8] for determination of the PSA. However, these methods are accurate but are expensive and time-consuming which hinder them to be suitable for clinical applications. Therefore, developing novel analytical methods are highly needed for rapid, sensitive, selective and low-cost determination of the PSA. Among the available methods, electrochemical aptasensors are obtaining a widespread attention due to having aptamers as their recognition element which cause some advantages such as high sensitivity and selectivity for aptasensors [9]. Aptamers as functional single-stranded oligonucleotides are isolated from random sequence of nucleic acid libraries by SELEX [10].

In fabrication of electrochemical aptasensors, a bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is usually chosen as the platform of the aptasensor which will be modified to obtain a better analytical performance. There are a variety of modifiers which can be used to modify the bare GCE. Nanomaterials have a very important position in modification of the bare GCE to develop electrochemical sensors and biosensors. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial have advantages such as high thermal conductivity, large surface area and high mobility of charge carriers which make them to be suitable in electrochemical detection [11–13]. Nafion (NAF) is a chemical inert sulfonated ion exchange polymer which is permeable and biocompatible with antifouling properties and due to its hydrophobicity is able to fix the carbon nanomaterial on the electrode surface because of its hydrophobicity [14,15]. NAF is also able to hinder aggregation of the carbon nanomaterials [16]. The main problem related to the pure NAF is related to its low conductivity which hinders electron transport [17,18]. To tackle this problem, NAF is often used in combibibion with an ionic liquid (IL). Chitosan (CS) as a natural biopolymer is biocompatible with good mechanical properties which has been frequently used in fabrication of electrochemical sensors and biosensors [19]. High surface area and high electron transfer rate of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have made them to be chosen as good modifier in electrochemical sensing [20]. Graphene (Gr) consists of a monolayer of sp2 bonded carbons which has a lot of applications in fuel cells, batteries and electrochemical sensors and biosensors due to its specific electronic, mechanical and thermal characteristics [21]. MoS2 decorated by metallic nanoparticles has been widely to construct electrochemical sensors because of its large surface area and excellent conductivity [22–25]. Bimetallic alloy nanoparticles have been widely used in electrochemical sensing because of the interactions of their metal components which make them to have favorable properties such as better catalytic activity and selectivity than their corresponding monometallic counterparts [19]. Therefore, decoration of MoS2 with bimetallic alloy nanoparticles is very interesting to develop novel electrochemical sensing platforms. Conducting polymers are very useful for improving the conductive properties and stability of the electrochemical sensors and have many applications in fabrication of electrochemical sensors and biosensors.

Nowadays, with the increasing role of computer in different scientific fields, electrochemical systems have also been affected by computerized methods which can help them to be more effective and interesting. Recently, a work has reported a novel software namely SACMES [26], which can help the electrochemists for the analysis and continuous monitoring of their electrochemical systems.

In this work, we are going to develop a novel computerized electrochemical system for determination of PSA. To achieve this goal, a bare GCE will be modified with graphene quantum dots-chitosan-nafion-ionic liquid/multiwalled carbon nanotubes-graphene-ionic liquid/polypyrrole-MoS2-ionic liquid-AuPt nanoparticles/aptamer (APT/Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE). The modifications will be characterized by electrochemical and microscopic techniques. Then, the response of the aptasensor will be processed by SACMES and after checking its analytical performance for determination of the PSA in synthetic samples, it will be applied to the determination of the PSA in real matrices. A summarized graphical description of the steps of our work is shown in Scheme 1.

Pyrrole, KCl, PSA, HSA, NSE, BSA, hemoglobin, thrombin, human immunoglobulin G (IgG) and lysozyme citric acid, 6-mercapto-1-hexanol, n-butyllithium (n-BuLi), Al2O3, LiClO4, MWCNTs, molybdenum disulfide, CS, Gr, NAF, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide (IL), potassium ferrocyanide, potassium ferriccyanide, gold (III) tetrachloride (HAuCl4), hexachloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6), dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid, sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), phosphoric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sigma. Thiol terminated PSA binding DNA aptamer (5′-HS-(CH2)6-TTTTTA ATT AAA GCT CGC CAT CAA ATA GCTTT-3′, APT) was synthesized by Takapu Zist Institute (Tehran, Iran). The other chemicals used in this project were purchased from well-known sources. Double distilled water (DDW) was used to prepare all the solutions.

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid [27], here, 1 g of citric acid was heated in a beaker to 200 °C for half an hour to obtain an orange liquid which then, was drop-by-drop added to 50 mL NaOH (10 mg mL−1) with continuous stirring. Then, the pH of the obtained GQD solution was adjusted to pH=8 and stored in the refrigerator. A phosphate buffered solution (PBS, 0.05 M, pH 7.4) was used to prepare the working solutions required in this project. An electrochemical probe solution ([Fe(CN)6]3-/4−) with a concentration of 0.05 M was prepared from K4[Fe(CN)6] and K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.05 M KCl. 10 mg CS was added to 0.5 mL acetic acid and 20 µL IL and 10 µL NAF were added to it and ultrasonicated for 1 h to obtain CS-NAF-IL. 0.5 mL GQDs was added to 0.5 mL CS-NAF-IL and ultrasonicated to obtain a homogeneous GQDs-CS-NAF-IL solution. A solution with a concentration of 0.1 M PSA was prepared in the PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.4). 500 mg MWCNTs were added to 1 mL DMF and ultrasonicated for 1 h. 300 mg Gr was added to DMF and ultrasonicated for 1 h. To prepare MWCNTs-Gr-IL solution, 20 µL IL was added to 490 µL MWCNTs+490 µL Gr. An ion intercalation-assisted liquid exfoliation procedure was used to exfoliation of MoS2 sheets by which tiny guest lithium atoms were penetrated into the MoS2 sandwich layers weakening interlayer interactions of MoS2. To prepare MoS2-IL, 50 mg MoS2 was added to 0.5 mL n-BuLi and well mixed by hand-shaking and then, 10 µL IL was added to it and the tube was covered and left at room temperature for 24 h. A solution containing 5 mM HAuCl4 and 5 mM H2PtCl6 was prepared in 0.1 M KCl. A solution of APT with a concentration of 10 µM was prepared in the PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.4).

An Autolab PGSTAT302N (Metrohm China Ltd, Switzerland) was used to record all the electrochemical data. A bare or modified GCE, an Ag/AgCl and a Pt wire were used as working, reference and counter electrode, respectively. An ELMEIRON pH-meter (CP-411) equipped with a combined glass electrode was used for pH adjustments. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were recorded by a FEI Quanta 450 scanning electron microscope. Elemental analysis of the modification towards detection of Mo, S, Au and Pt was performed by an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) with the help of mentioned SEM instrument. SACMES was obtained upon our request to its owners. Computations based on asymmetric least squares splines regression (AsLSSR) and elliptical joint confidence region (EJCR) were performed in MATLAB environment. All the computations were performed on a Dell XPS laptop.

First of all, the bare GCE (surface area 6.1 µm2) was well-polished on a silky pad impregnated with alumina slurry and then, put in a beaker containing ethanol and ultrasonicated for 30 min. The GCE was then rinsed with DDW. The cleaned GCE was covered until using time. The procedure was repeated whenever washing the GCE was needed. 12 µL GQDs-CS-NAF-IL was drop-casted onto the surface of the cleaned GCE and left to be dried at room temperature. By drop-casting of 10 µL of MWCNTs-Gr-IL onto the surface of GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, a thick film was formed onto its surface to fabricate MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE and then, pyrrole was electropolymerized onto its surface by cyclic voltammetry (CV, 30 cycles). After forming the Ppy onto the surface of MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, 10 µL MoS2-IL was drop-casted onto its surface and then, the electrode was immersed into the solution containing 5 mM HAuCl4, 5 mM H2PtCl6 and by cycling potential from 0 to −1 for 10 times AuPt NPs were electrodeposited onto the electrode surface. The Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE was immersed into the APT solution (10 µM prepared in the PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.4)) for 12 h and then, the electrode was rinsed with PBS which was further treated with 1 mM 6-mercapto-1-hexanol for 1 h to obtain a well aligned aptamer monolayer. Finally, the APT/Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE was rinsed with PBS and DDW to remove excess aptamer molecules.
Three human serum samples were prepared from a diagnostic laboratory which were centrifuged and diluted (1:10) with the PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.4) and stored frozen until analysis time.

In fabrication of an electrochemical aptasensor it is usual to characterize the modifications applied to its platform prior to its application to the analysis and determination of the analyte of interest. Therefore, in this section of our work, we are going to characterize the modifications applied to fabricate the aptasensor which will be explained in more details in next sections.

Microscopic characterization of the surface of the aptasensor can help us for effective verification of the fabrication procedure applied to the bare GCE. SEM is a very useful technique which can help us to monitor the surface of the electrode therefore, we have used it for characterization of the surface of the sensor and its images are shown in Fig. 1 .

Fig. 1A shows the surface of the GCE which is flat and clean and Fig. 1B shows the SEM image of the surface of GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE which shows that the CS has formed layers on them the GQDs can be seen. As can be seen, a composite film has covered the electrode surface. After modification of the GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE with MWCNTs-Gr-IL, a new layer is formed in which tubes of the MWCNTs and sheets of the graphene have twined around each other (Fig. 1C). Electropolymerization of the pyrrole onto the electrode surface could be clearly seen in Fig. 1D which provides a new and stable platform for the next modification steps. Landing of the MoS2 nanosheets on the electrode surface was the next step of the modification process which can be seen in Fig. 1E. Decoration of the surface of MoS2 nanosheets with AuPt NPs is shown in Fig. 1F.

The next attempt for further characterization of the modification process was devoted to the application of the EDS for elemental analysis of Au, Pt, Mo and S and the results are shown in Fig. 1G. As can be seen, signature peaks related to Au, Pt, Mo and S were detected which also confirmed successfulness of the modification process.

Electrochemical techniques such as CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) are very effective and useful techniques which have been frequently used to monitor the modifications applied to fabricate electrochemical sensors and biosensors [28–35]. Therefore, we are going to use them for the electrochemical characterization of the modifications used to fabricate the aptasensor.

Fig. 2 A shows the EISs of different electrodes in contact with 0.05 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4−. As can be seen, the GCE had an EIS response (curve a) with a dimeter of 872 Ω which its resistance was increased to 1140 Ω after its modification with GQDs-NAF (curve b) which was related to the electrostatic repulsion of the electrochemical probe molecules carrying negative charges from the electrode surface due to the presence of NAF. Integration of GQDs with IL and its drop-casting onto the GCE surface had a sensible effect on its response which forced its resistance to be decreased (726 Ω, curve c) which was related to the more conductivity obtained by the presence of IL. The presence of GQDs-NAF-IL at the electrode surface caused a little effect on resistance of the electrode surface (curve d) but by the presence of GQDs-CS-NAF-IL at the electrode surface an observable decreasing of the charge transfer resistance (R ct, curve e) was observed. Presence of MWCNTs-IL, MWCNTs-Gr-IL, Ppy, Ppy-MoS2-IL, Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs at the electrode surface caused facilitating of charge transfer at the electrode surface leading to decreasing the charge transfer resistance as can be seen from curves f-j. After incubation of Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE with APT, a higher R ct of 1500 Ω was observed which confirmed that the aptamers inhibit the access of electrons to the electrode surface leading to low electron-transfer efficiency of the system [36].

Further characterization of the modifications used to fabricate the aptasensor was performed by CV as an important electrochemical technique where variation in the response of different modified electrodes to 0.05 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- was used to justify the modification steps and the results are shown in Fig. 2B. As can be seen, the GCE had a well-shape response (curve a) which was affected by the presence of GQDs-NAF on its surface (curve b). As can be seen, the currents were decreased and peak separation was increased which confirmed a weaker response for GQDs-NAF/GCE than bare GCE. The CV response of GQDs-IL/GCE was improved (curve c) which was related to the contribution of IL. A little improvement in the CV response of GQDs-NAF-IL/GCE was observed which (curve d) which was remarkably improved by the presence of GQDs-CS-NAF-IL (curve e). The last layer was conductive so enough to obtain a good response and was also a stable composite for landing the next layers. Landing of MWCNTs-IL was the next attempt for the modification of the electrode (curve f) which its impact was further improved by its combination with Gr as can be seen in curve g. The response of MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE was further improved by the presence of Ppy, Ppy-MoS2-IL and Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs on its surface as can be seen by curve h, i and j, respectively. By the incubation of Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE with APT, a weaker response was observed because of the hindrance of the electrons to access the electrode surface due to the presence of APTs. As can be seen, the results of CV and EIS experiments confirmed each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of different aptasensors for selecting the best one for detection of the PSA
ce:section.ce:para: In developing electroanalytical systems, it is usual to investigate and optimize the effects of the variables which can affect the response of the system. Therefore, in this section, we are going to examine the response of different modified electrodes for choosing the best electrode for detection of PSA for our next studies. Performance of GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE, MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE and Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE was examined by EIS and DPV and the results are shown in Figs. S1 and S2, respectively. The EIS curves showed that by the presence of APTs onto the surface of the electrodes, the R ct values were increased which confirmed that the APTs inhibited the access of the electrons to the electrode surface in some extent which led to increase the R ct values. By binding of PSAs on the electrodes′ surface, the R ct values were further increased which were related to the steric hindrance generated by the PSAs. The DPV data were in a good accordance with EIS data which confirmed their results. The DPV data confirmed that by the presence of APTs onto the surface of the electrodes, the peak heights were decreased which showed that the access of the electrons to the electrode surface could be limited in some extent. Binding of PSAs on the electrodes′ surface caused some steric hindrance which caused the intensities of the DPV peaks to be decreased. For the assessment of the performance of the tested aptasensors, ΔR ct=R PSA-R APT and ΔI=I PSA -I APT were calculated for each aptasensor which can be seen in Fig. S3. The highest ΔR ct and ΔI were related to Ppy-MoS2-IL-AuPt NPs/MWCNTs-Gr-IL/GQDs-CS-NAF-IL/GCE which was related to its effective modification which motivated us as the most effective aptasensor for analytical purposes.

For application of the aptasensor to the analytical purposes, its response to the PSA must be calibrated. Therefore, to achieve this goal, we have done a step for obtaining a linear range of PSA concentration in which the aptasensor response was linearly correlated with PSA concentration. Here, square wave voltammetry (SWV) was used for calibrating the aptasensor response. The SWV data for each sample were recorded eight times at different frequencies ranging in 5–40 Hz. After data acquisition by the Autolab, Fig. 3
A, they were processed by SACMES to build a calibration curve as can be seen in Fig. 3B. To obtain a linear relation between area under curve (AUC) values and PSA concentration which could be used to predict PSA concentration in unknown samples, the AUC values were regressed on PSA concentration (0.5–350 pg mL−1) as can be seen in Fig. 3C. Limit of the detection (LOD) of the developed method was calculated according to the IUPAC recommendation, 3S b/m, which was 0.14 pg mL−1 (where S b is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration curve). Our records confirmed a linear range of PSA concentration including 0.5–350 pg mL−1 with a LOD of 0.14 pg mL−1 were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of the interference effects on aptasensor response
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the selectivity of the fabricated aptasensor, its response to 50 ng mL−1 PSA was recorded in the presence of 50-fold concentration of HSA, NSE, hemoglobin, thrombin, IgG and lysozyme. To achieve this goal, the aptasensor response was recorded in blank, 25 µg mL−1 HSA, NSE, hemoglobin, thrombin, human IgG and lysozyme and 50 ng mL−1 PSA and the results are shown in Fig. S4A. As can be seen, the aptasensor responses to HSA, NSE, hemoglobin, thrombin, human IgG, mixture (HSA, NSE, hemoglobin, thrombin, human IgG) and lysozyme are nearly same as its response to blank. While, the aptasensor response to 50 ng mL−1 PSA is clearly different which confirms that the aptasensor has a good selectivity towards PSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability and reproducibility of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Stability of an aptasensor as a device which is going to be suggested for the analytical purposes is a key parameter which must be clarified. Therefore, we clarified the stability of the aptasensor by its application to the determination of 50 ng mL−1 PSA during one month. As can be seen, the aptasensor was able to retain 94% of its original response to 50 ng mL−1 PSA which was a great evidence to confirm an acceptable stability for the aptasensor, Fig. S4B. Another characteristic of the aptasensor which was verified was related to the examination of its repeatability and to achieve this goal, we have applied it to the determination of 50 ng mL−1 PSA for seven times during a day. Our results showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8% which confirmed the repeatability of the aptasensor response. Further verification of the operating characteristic of the aptasensor was focused on its reproducibility by fabrication of six aptasensors upon the same procedure and their application to the determination of 50 ng mL−1 PSA. Our records showed a RSD of 3.35% which confirmed the reproducibility of the aptasensor response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the aptasensor to the analysis of the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A very important step in developing and introducing an analytical method is related to its application to the analysis of real samples towards determination of the analyte of interest. Therefore, this section of our study was dedicated to the examination of the performance of the developed aptasensor for determination of the PSA in real samples. To achieve this goal, by the same procedure used for calibration step, the aptasensor responses in serum samples were recorded. The results of the analyses of the real samples have been collected in Table 1 . As can be seen, good recoveries confirmed the successfulness of the fabricated aptasensor for the analysis of real samples towards determination of the PSA. For further investigation of the accuracy and precision of the developed aptasensor in this work, EJCR was used to do linear regression of the predicted concentrations (found) on the nominal (added) ones and the results are shown in Fig. S4C. A smaller ellipse shows more precision of the developed analytical method and containing the ideal point by the ellipse shows the accuracy of the developed analytical method. As can be seen, the fabricated aptasensor had good precision and accuracy in the analysis of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with the previous works reported in the literature
ce:section.ce:para: A look was casted on the literature to provide some information about the previous works published in the literature on determination of the PSA to compare the performance of them with the fabricated aptasensor in this work. The results have been collected in Table S1. As can be seen, our aptasensor had a much better performance than the previous reported aptasensors. Application of a good modification procedure to fabricate the aptasensor and coupling of its outputs with SACMES helped us to fabricate a novel aptasensor with better practical characteristics than the previous reported aptasensors.

In this work, a novel, sensitive and computerized monitoring system was developed for determination of PSA based on fabrication of a novel aptasensor and its coupling with SACMES as an efficient computerized monitoring system. Step-by-step modification of the bare GCE by different modifiers led us to develop a very interesting aptasensor for determination of the PSA which was also assisted by a computerized monitoring system. The developed system had a sensitive, selective, stable, repeatable and reproducible response which was able to the determination of the PSA in real samples with acceptable results. This work introduces a very interesting study which can be more expanded for developing more interesting studies in near future.

Shokoufeh Soleimani: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Data curation. Elham Arkan: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Ali R. Jalalvand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft. Hector C. Goicoechea: Writing - review & editing, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by research council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences and we acknowledge that.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104898.
